The **Atlantic Branch** is an [electrified](/wiki/Railway_electrification "Railway electrification") [rail line](/wiki/Track_(rail_transport) "Track (rail transport)") owned and operated by the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). It is the only LIRR line with revenue passenger service in the borough of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn").

The line consists of two sections constructed separately. The portion of the line from Atlantic Terminal to Jamaica was constructed as part of the [Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_and_Jamaica_Railroad "Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad") and opened in 1836, while the portion from Jamaica to Valley Stream was constructed as part of the [South Side Railroad of Long Island](/wiki/South_Side_Railroad_of_Long_Island "South Side Railroad of Long Island") in 1867.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Atlantic_Branch "Special:EditPage/Atlantic Branch") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(August 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Partly underground and partly elevated, the Atlantic Branch runs from [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") to [Valley Stream](/wiki/Valley_Stream,_New_York "Valley Stream, New York"), in [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York"), where it becomes the two-track [Long Beach Branch](/wiki/Long_Beach_Branch "Long Beach Branch") with the two-track [Far Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch "Far Rockaway Branch") splitting southward just east of the [Valley Stream station](/wiki/Valley_Stream_(LIRR_station) "Valley Stream (LIRR station)").

The section between Atlantic Terminal and [Bedford Avenue](/wiki/Bedford_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Bedford Avenue (Brooklyn)") is underground along [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Atlantic Avenue (Brooklyn)"). From there the line is elevated above the [median](/wiki/Median_(highway) "Median (highway)") of Atlantic Avenue to Dewey Place (with a stop at [Nostrand Avenue](/wiki/Nostrand_Avenue_(LIRR_station) "Nostrand Avenue (LIRR station)")) before returning underground. At [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York_(LIRR_station) "East New York (LIRR station)") the line rises to street level to cross above the north–south, freight-only [Bay Ridge Branch](/wiki/Bay_Ridge_Branch "Bay Ridge Branch"), then descends underground once more. Between East New York and [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_(LIRR_station) "Jamaica (LIRR station)"), the closed but intact station at [Woodhaven Junction](/wiki/Woodhaven_Junction_(LIRR_station) "Woodhaven Junction (LIRR station)") is visible.

At 121st Street in [Richmond Hill, Queens](/wiki/Richmond_Hill,_Queens "Richmond Hill, Queens"), the line rises to street level and passes the [Morris Park Facility](/wiki/Morris_Park_Facility "Morris Park Facility") before joining the elevated [Main Line](/wiki/Main_Line_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Main Line (Long Island Rail Road)") at Jamaica. Immediately east of Jamaica, the line turns southeast, ducking beneath the eastward Main Line tracks. It curves parallel to the [Montauk Branch](/wiki/Montauk_Branch "Montauk Branch") after a few miles and continues next to it to [Valley Interlocking](/wiki/Valley_Stream_(LIRR_station) "Valley Stream (LIRR station)") in Valley Stream.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Woodhaven_Junc_Power_Sta_3_jeh.JPG/250px-Woodhaven_Junc_Power_Sta_3_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Woodhaven_Junc_Power_Sta_3_jeh.JPG)

[Woodhaven Junction](/wiki/Woodhaven_Junction "Woodhaven Junction") power substation

The current Atlantic Branch is the successor to two separate lines: the [Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_and_Jamaica_Railroad "Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad") (opened 1836) along Atlantic Avenue from Flatbush Avenue to Jamaica, and the [South Side Railroad of Long Island](/wiki/South_Side_Railroad_of_Long_Island "South Side Railroad of Long Island") (opened 1867) from Jamaica to Valley Stream.

### Atlantic Terminal to Jamaica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Atlantic Terminal to Jamaica")]

See also: [Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_and_Jamaica_Railroad "Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad")

The [Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_and_Jamaica_Railroad "Brooklyn and Jamaica Railroad") opened the line from [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry,_Brooklyn "South Ferry, Brooklyn") to what is now 151st Street in Jamaica on April 18, 1836. The line was leased to the Long Island Rail Road on December 1 that year. Upon the opening of the Hunter's Point terminal of the LIRR at Long Island City, all LIRR traffic was diverted to that terminal, and the Atlantic Avenue line, now owned solely by the Brooklyn Central and Jamaica Railroad, installed horse-drawn trains through Brooklyn (in response to a law banning steam in the city) and steam service from the city line to Jamaica. In 1867, the LIRR leased the line east of Classon Avenue, running its own steam service on this section. In 1876, the restriction of steam on Atlantic Avenue was lifted, and the line was leased east of Flatbush Avenue to the LIRR on June 1, 1877, with LIRR steam service beginning anew on June 28, 1877.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Initially the line turned halfway between Classon and Franklin Avenues, running halfway between Herkimer Street and Schuyler Street (now Atlantic Avenue) along the line of the present Herkimer Place. It turned slightly to the southeast near Howard Avenue, crossing the centerline of Schuyler Street about one-third of the way between Hopkinson Avenue (Thomas Boyland Street) and Paca Avenue (Rockaway Avenue). It crossed into the town of [New Lots](/wiki/New_Lots,_Brooklyn "New Lots, Brooklyn") just beyond Stone Avenue (Mother Gaston Boulevard).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Atlantic Branch was one of the first lines in the LIRR system slated to be electrified. In anticipation of this the entire line to Jamaica was to be grade separated. Between 1903 and 1905 the line was depressed into a tunnel from Flatbush Avenue to Bedford Avenue, then placed on an elevated viaduct from Bedford Avenue to Ralph Avenue then depressed back into a tunnel until Manhattan Crossing located just west of East New York station. At East New York the line returned to grade level then rose onto another elevated viaduct until Atkins Ave. The rest of the line from Atkins Ave to Morris Park located just west of Jamaica remained at grade level along Atlantic Avenue with numerous grade crossings with the anticipation of grade separating the line later on. Additionally a new terminal and yard was built at Flatbush and Atlantic Avenues. Electric service commenced in 1905 with the line consisting of two tracks between Flatbush Avenue and [Woodhaven Junction](/wiki/Woodhaven_Junction "Woodhaven Junction") and four tracks beyond that point to Jamaica.

LIRR then ran two services along the line: the traditional commuter type services from points on eastern Long Island to Flatbush Avenue, along with what was called the "local [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit")" service, frequent elevated/subway like service at lower fare between Flatbush Ave and [Queens Village](/wiki/Queens_Village_station "Queens Village station"). Although referred to as a rapid transit service, standard LIRR cars were used, and the service was operated by regular railroad rules. At this time the line from Jamaica to East New York had many more stations along Atlantic Avenue spaced at closer intervals, much like a rapid transit line. The four tracks between Jamaica and Woodhaven Junction lent itself to this service with the "rapid transit" trains using the outer two tracks while commuter trains used the inner two tracks.

In November 1925, 25 "local" trains left Brooklyn each weekday for Queens Village, 12 more ran to Hillside, and 16 more ran to Jamaica. All trains made all stops, 15 of them west of Queens Village. Fare was probably 10 cents for 13 miles Queens Village to Brooklyn, compared to about 40 cents on "express" LIRR trains making six or seven stops (but a monthly ticket good on any train was $7.10).

For a while the LIRR operated joint service along the Atlantic Branch with the [Brooklyn Rapid Transit](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit "Brooklyn Rapid Transit") company (BRT) consisting of two connections, one with the [Fifth Ave El](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue_Line_(Brooklyn_elevated) "Fifth Avenue Line (Brooklyn elevated)") at Flatbush Avenue, and another with the [Broadway](/wiki/BMT_Jamaica_Line "BMT Jamaica Line") and [Lexington Avenue els](/wiki/BMT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "BMT Lexington Avenue Line") with a connection built at Chestnut Street in Brooklyn. This allowed BRT trains to access the Rockaways and Manhattan Beach, while affording the LIRR a connection into Manhattan to the BRT terminal located at [Park Row](/wiki/Park_Row_(BMT_station) "Park Row (BMT station)") over the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") (this service predated the opening of the [East River Tunnels](/wiki/East_River_Tunnels "East River Tunnels") to [Penn Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)")). Nevertheless, the [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission") ended this service in 1916 when they classified different operating standards between rapid transit trains (such as BRT trains) and regular heavy rail railroads (such as the LIRR).

By the late 1930s, it was clear that the rest of the line needed to be grade separated. Much of the surrounding area along Atlantic Avenue in [Ozone Park](/wiki/Ozone_Park "Ozone Park") and [Richmond Hill](/wiki/Richmond_Hill,_Queens "Richmond Hill, Queens") began their suburban development leading to more traffic along Atlantic Avenue which was plagued by the line's many grade crossings. The City of New York along with the LIRR thus allocated the funds to depress the rest of the line from Morris Park to East New York in a tunnel. Building of the tunnel commenced in 1939 (although plans to build the tunnel date back to 1893) with two of the line's four tracks being pulled out of service and the rapid transit service being discontinued.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/NostrandAveViaductReplacement.jpg/250px-NostrandAveViaductReplacement.jpg)](/wiki/File:NostrandAveViaductReplacement.jpg)

Replacement of the Atlantic Avenue viaduct at Nostrand Avenue in July 2011

On December 28, 1942,[[4]](#cite_note-AtlanticAveLIRR-4) the tunnel was completed and opened with the two remaining at-grade tracks pulled out of service. Around this time Atlantic Avenue was raised over the East New York station via a viaduct that separated the road and the railroad. The elevated trestle from East New York to Atkins Avenue was also demolished as it had been included in the new tunnel to Jamaica. Only one station was included in the new tunnel: Woodhaven Junction, where the Atlantic Branch crossed under the [Rockaway Beach Branch](/wiki/Rockaway_Beach_Branch "Rockaway Beach Branch"). An interlocking and track connection was built just west of the Woodhaven Junction station to connect the two lines, but these closed after the abandonment of the Rockaway Beach Branch between 1955 and 1962.

### Jamaica to Valley Stream

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Jamaica to Valley Stream")]

See also: [South Side Railroad of Long Island](/wiki/South_Side_Railroad_of_Long_Island "South Side Railroad of Long Island")

The portion east of Jamaica was opened by the [South Side Railroad of Long Island](/wiki/South_Side_Railroad_of_Long_Island "South Side Railroad of Long Island") on October 28, 1867, as part of its initial line from Jamaica to [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon_(village),_New_York "Babylon (village), New York"). With the consolidation of the South Side into the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") system in 1876, all passenger trains were rerouted to use the LIRR main line from [Berlin Junction](/w/index.php?title=Berlin_Junction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Berlin Junction (page does not exist)") (west of Jamaica) to [Rockaway Junction](/wiki/Rockaway_Junction "Rockaway Junction") and the LIRR's [Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Cedarhurst_Cutoff "Cedarhurst Cutoff") to [Springfield Junction](/wiki/Springfield_Junction_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Springfield Junction (Long Island Rail Road)"), where it crossed the South Side. This change took effect June 25, 1876, and resulted in the closure of the South Side's Berlin, Beaver Street (Jamaica), [Locust Avenue](/wiki/Locust_Avenue_(LIRR_station) "Locust Avenue (LIRR station)"), and [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield_(LIRR_Atlantic_Branch_station) "Springfield (LIRR Atlantic Branch station)") stations.[[5]](#cite_note-Seyfried-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) This formed the current configuration, where the [Montauk Branch](/wiki/Montauk_Branch "Montauk Branch") follows this route, mostly ex-South Side, and the Atlantic Branch (then the **Old Southern Road**) uses the old South Side to Springfield Junction.

The line was soon reopened due to a lawsuit, but closed again by [Austin Corbin](/wiki/Austin_Corbin "Austin Corbin") as of January 6, 1881.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Effective May 17, 1906, when an [electrified](/wiki/Electrified_(rail) "Electrified (rail)") third track opened alongside the [Montauk Division](/wiki/Montauk_Division "Montauk Division") from [Springfield Junction](/wiki/Springfield_Junction_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Springfield Junction (Long Island Rail Road)") to Valley Stream, the Old Southern Road and this new track became part of the Atlantic Division.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Grade-crossing elimination work between Laurelton and Jamaica began in May 1958.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stations")]

East of Valley Stream, the Far Rockaway Branch continues to [Far Rockaway](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_station_(LIRR) "Far Rockaway station (LIRR)") and the Long Beach Branch continues to [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach_station_(LIRR) "Long Beach station (LIRR)").

As of February 27, 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_Branch&action=edit), the Atlantic Terminal, Nostrand Avenue, and East New York stations are primarily served by a shuttle running between Atlantic Terminal and Jamaica. These stations are also served by trains on the West Hempstead Branch, as well as a limited number of weekday trains on the Hempstead and Babylon branches. Other trains traveling east of Jamaica run to [Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station"), [Grand Central Madison](/wiki/Grand_Central_Madison "Grand Central Madison"), or [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City_station "Long Island City station").[[10]](#cite_note-ABC7_New_York_20230227-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Castillo_20230227-11)
